matches.author: Jay
matches.federalistPassage: FAREWELL! A LONG FAREWELL TO ALL MY GREATNESS.
matches.shakespearePassage: Farewell? A long farewell to all my greatness! / This is the state of man: today he puts forth / The tender leaves of hopes; tomorrow blossoms / And bears his blushing honors thick upon him; / The third day comes a frost, a killing frost
matches.explanation: Jay explicitly signals he is quoting by writing "in the words of the poet." Cardinal Wolsey's soliloquy on losing power is applied to America's potential loss of greatness through disunion. The only openly acknowledged Shakespeare quotation in the entire Federalist Papers.

matches.author: Jay
matches.federalistPassage: They who have turned their attention to the affairs of men, must have perceived that there are tides in them
matches.shakespearePassage: There is a tide in the affairs of men / Which, taken at the flood, leads on to fortune
matches.explanation: A clear, deliberate allusion to Brutus's speech. The structural parallel ("affairs of men...tides in them") goes well beyond proverbial usage. Jay restructures the quotation but preserves the key vocabulary and political argument.

matches.author: Jay
matches.federalistPassage: an inheritance so proper and convenient for a band of brethren, united to each other by the strongest ties
matches.shakespearePassage: We few, we happy few, we band of brothers; / For he today that sheds his blood with me / Shall be my brother
matches.explanation: The phrase "band of brethren" closely echoes Shakespeare's famous "band of brothers" from the St. Crispin's Day speech. Jay uses it in a strikingly parallel context of national unity. However, the phrase also has biblical resonance and was not exclusively Shakespearean by the 1780s.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: Our ambassadors abroad are the mere pageants of mimic sovereignty.
matches.shakespearePassage: And, like this insubstantial pageant faded, / Leave not a rack behind (Tempest); A woeful pageant have we here beheld (R2)
matches.explanation: The word "pageant" meaning a hollow show or empty display is distinctly Shakespearean in its resonance. Shakespeare uses it repeatedly to mean a performance that is visually impressive but lacking substance. Hamilton's "mere pageants of mimic sovereignty" precisely matches this Shakespearean sense.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: any matter still in the womb of time and experience
matches.shakespearePassage: There are many events in the womb of time which will be delivered.
matches.explanation: The phrase "womb of time" is distinctively Shakespearean, coined or popularized by Shakespeare in Othello. The phrase appears nowhere in pre-Shakespearean English with this meaning. By Hamilton's era it had entered educated English diction more broadly, so this may be absorbed idiom rather than deliberate allusion.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: the multitudinous composition of that body
matches.shakespearePassage: The multitudinous seas incarnadine (Macbeth); The multitudinous tongue (Coriolanus)
matches.explanation: Shakespeare's distinctive coinage. The word "multitudinous" was sufficiently rare and sufficiently associated with Shakespeare to constitute a meaningful linguistic marker. Hamilton uses it in a political context nearly identical to Coriolanus.

matches.author: Madison
matches.federalistPassage: the more multitudinous a representative assembly may be rendered
matches.shakespearePassage: The multitudinous tongue (Coriolanus); The multitudinous seas incarnadine (Macbeth)
matches.explanation: Same distinctive Shakespearean word used in a political context directly parallel to Coriolanus, where "the multitudinous tongue" refers to the power of the popular voice in assemblies.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: if the malcontents had been headed by a Caesar or by a Cromwell
matches.shakespearePassage: Caesar as archetype of ambitious tyranny pervades the play, from his ambition to the conspiracy against him.
matches.explanation: Hamilton invokes "a Caesar" as a type -- the ambitious military leader who subverts a republic. Shakespeare's Julius Caesar fixed this figure most powerfully in the Anglo-American literary imagination. The pairing with Cromwell suggests Hamilton is drawing on a tradition of republican cautionary figures.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: The ambitious cardinal, who was prime minister to Henry VIII., permitting his vanity to aspire to the triple crown
matches.shakespearePassage: Thy ambition, / Thou scarlet sin, robbed this bewailing land (Surrey); Cromwell, I charge thee, fling away ambition! / By that sin fell the angels (Wolsey)
matches.explanation: Hamilton's historical example of Wolsey's dangerous ambition draws on the same episode Shakespeare dramatized in Henry VIII. An educated 18th-century reader would almost certainly recall Shakespeare's dramatization. However, Hamilton is using Wolsey as a historical example and his source may be chronicle as much as literary.

matches.author: Disputed (Hamilton or Madison)
matches.federalistPassage: Ambition must be made to counteract ambition.
matches.shakespearePassage: Ambition should be made of sterner stuff (JC 3.2); Vaulting ambition, which o'erleaps itself (Macbeth 1.7)
matches.explanation: The Federalist author's formulation shares with Shakespeare not just the key word but a distinctive rhetorical structure: ambition as a force that must be structurally channeled. The verbal echo of "Ambition...made" from Julius Caesar 3.2 is notable. Also has roots in Machiavelli and Hume.

matches.author: Disputed (Hamilton or Madison)
matches.federalistPassage: In all very numerous assemblies, of whatever character composed, passion never fails to wrest the sceptre from reason.
matches.shakespearePassage: Then everything includes itself in power, / Power into will, will into appetite -- describing what happens when degree (reason) is overthrown.
matches.explanation: The image of passion unseating reason as a ruler (wresting the "sceptre") is strikingly Shakespearean. Ulysses' degree speech describes precisely this process. The conceptual architecture -- reason as a monarch vulnerable to overthrow by passion -- is characteristically Shakespearean, though also found in Locke and others.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: Whenever two or more persons are engaged in any common enterprise or pursuit, there is always danger of difference of opinion...dissension...personal emulation and even the animal hostility of individual predominance
matches.shakespearePassage: When two authorities are up, / Neither supreme, how soon confusion / May enter 'twixt the gap of both
matches.explanation: Hamilton's extended argument against dual executive authority closely tracks Coriolanus 3.1's warning about divided authority. The structural parallel is strong: both argue that dual authority inherently produces factional conflict and paralysis.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: The judiciary...has no influence over either the sword or the purse...It may truly be said to have neither FORCE nor WILL, but merely judgment
matches.shakespearePassage: The play explores what happens when judicial authority is separated from or reunited with executive power, and when "man, proud man, / Dressed in a little brief authority" (2.2) exercises judgment.
matches.explanation: Hamilton's foundational description of the judiciary as having "neither FORCE nor WILL, but merely judgment" engages with the central problem of Measure for Measure: the relationship between law's authority and its enforcement. Both texts explore how judgment operates without direct coercive power.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: without an easy access to exceptions in favor of unfortunate guilt, justice would wear a countenance too sanguinary and cruel
matches.shakespearePassage: The quality of mercy is not strained...It is an attribute to God himself; / And earthly power doth then show likest God's / When mercy seasons justice
matches.explanation: Hamilton's argument for presidential pardoning power -- that strict justice without mercy becomes cruelty -- is Portia's argument in Merchant of Venice. The specific framing of mercy as a prerogative that makes the ruler greater was the most famous literary treatment available to Hamilton.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: a dangerous ambition more often lurks behind the specious mask of zeal for the rights of the people
matches.shakespearePassage: 'Tis a common proof / That lowliness is young ambition's ladder (JC); I have no spur / To prick the sides of my intent, but only / Vaulting ambition (Macbeth)
matches.explanation: Hamilton's warning engages with the Shakespearean theme of ambition masked by public virtue. The thematic resonance is real but this is also a broadly classical theme (Sallust, Tacitus, Plutarch). No close verbal parallel.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: of those men who have overturned the liberties of republics, the greatest number have begun their career by paying an obsequious court to the people; commencing demagogues, and ending tyrants.
matches.shakespearePassage: The tribunes' manipulation of citizens in Coriolanus; Antony's demagogic funeral oration in Julius Caesar 3.2; Caesar's rise through popular adulation.
matches.explanation: Hamilton's capsule narrative -- demagogue to tyrant -- is a great theme of Shakespeare's Roman plays. However, the trajectory is a classical commonplace (Aristotle, Polybius, Plutarch) that Shakespeare inherited rather than invented.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: A torrent of angry and malignant passions will be let loose.
matches.shakespearePassage: Cry 'Havoc!' and let slip the dogs of war, / That this foul deed shall smell above the earth / With carrion men groaning for burial
matches.explanation: Hamilton's imagery of destructive passions being "let loose" loosely parallels Antony's prophecy. The verb "let loose" and "let slip" are near-synonyms applied to similar political imagery. However, "let loose" was a common phrase.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: Is it not time to awake from the deceitful dream of a golden age
matches.shakespearePassage: I would with such perfection govern, sir, / T' excel the Golden Age. (Gonzalo)
matches.explanation: Both Shakespeare and Hamilton treat the Golden Age as a dangerous illusion that ignores human self-interest. However, the Golden Age is a classical concept from Ovid and Hesiod, not distinctly Shakespearean.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: The airy phantoms that flit before the distempered imaginations of some of its adversaries
matches.shakespearePassage: And, like this insubstantial pageant faded, / Leave not a rack behind (Tempest); all the interim is / Like a phantasma (JC)
matches.explanation: The language of unreality shares a register with Shakespeare's treatment of illusion and reality. However, "airy phantoms" was not uniquely Shakespearean and the connection is atmospheric rather than textual.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: the wretched nurseries of unceasing discord
matches.shakespearePassage: Take but degree away, untune that string, / And hark what discord follows
matches.explanation: Hamilton's argument that small republics breed discord engages with the Shakespearean theme that division breeds chaos (Ulysses' degree speech). However, Hamilton's argument derives explicitly from Montesquieu.

matches.author: Madison
matches.federalistPassage: The instability, injustice, and confusion introduced into the public councils, have, in truth, been the mortal diseases under which popular governments have everywhere perished
matches.shakespearePassage: When two authorities are up, / Neither supreme, how soon confusion / May enter (Cor.); the enterprise is sick (T&C)
matches.explanation: Madison's core argument aligns with Shakespeare's treatment of faction, and the disease metaphor parallels Shakespeare's characterization of political disorder as illness. However, Madison's argument is grounded in Hume, and the metaphor goes back to Aristotle.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: the frail and tottering edifice seems ready to fall upon our heads, and to crush us beneath its ruins
matches.shakespearePassage: Ulysses' degree speech on the collapse of political structures when their organizing principle fails.
matches.explanation: Hamilton's image of the confederacy as a failing building echoes a Shakespearean pattern of political structures as physical edifices that collapse. However, the metaphor of the state as an edifice is classical.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: conducted us to the brink of a precipice, seem resolved to plunge us into the abyss that awaits us below
matches.shakespearePassage: The Dover cliff scene in King Lear; Gloucester at the precipice.
matches.explanation: Hamilton's precipice/abyss imagery resonates with Shakespeare's recurring use of cliffs, falls, and abysses as metaphors for political catastrophe. However, this is common rhetorical imagery.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: When the sword is once drawn, the passions of men observe no bounds of moderation.
matches.shakespearePassage: Cry 'Havoc!' and let slip the dogs of war; Domestic fury and fierce civil strife / Shall cumber all the parts of Italy
matches.explanation: Hamilton's observation about war's uncontrollable escalation resonates with Antony's prophecy. However, this is a general truth found in Thucydides, Machiavelli, and many others. No specifically Shakespearean language.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: how unequal parchment provisions are to a struggle with public necessity
matches.shakespearePassage: inky blots and rotten parchment bonds (R2); Is not this a lamentable thing, that of the skin of an innocent lamb should be made parchment? (H6P2)
matches.explanation: Hamilton's contemptuous "parchment provisions" echoes Shakespeare's skepticism about written law vs. actual power. However, "parchment" as synecdoche for written law was common 18th-century political rhetoric.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: seditions and insurrections are, unhappily, maladies as inseparable from the body politic as tumors and eruptions from the natural body
matches.shakespearePassage: There was a time when all the body's members / Rebelled against the belly... (the fable of the belly)
matches.explanation: The body politic metaphor is one of the oldest in Western political thought. While Shakespeare gave it famous literary expressions, Hamilton's usage is standard political rhetoric drawing from shared classical tradition.

matches.author: Hamilton and Madison
matches.federalistPassage: renders the empire a nerveless body, incapable of regulating its own members, insecure against external dangers, and agitated with unceasing fermentations in its own bowels
matches.shakespearePassage: The body politic tradition, including the belly fable in Coriolanus and Canterbury's honeybee speech in Henry V.
matches.explanation: The image of the state as a "nerveless body" with "fermentations in its own bowels" continues the body politic tradition. Conventional political language.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: Money is, with propriety, considered as the vital principle of the body politic; as that which sustains its life and motion
matches.shakespearePassage: Same body politic tradition. See Coriolanus (the belly fable) and Henry V (Canterbury's speech).
matches.explanation: Hamilton extends the body politic metaphor. Shakespeare is not the specific source; the metaphor had been commonplace for centuries.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: Imagination may range at pleasure till it gets bewildered amidst the labyrinths of an enchanted castle
matches.shakespearePlay: Troilus and Cressida; Venus and Adonis
matches.shakespearePassage: labyrinth of thy fury (T&C); Are like a labyrinth to amaze his foes (V&A)
matches.explanation: The image of "enchanted castle" and "labyrinths" belongs more to Spenserian romance than to Shakespeare. Shakespeare used "labyrinth" but did not originate the tradition Hamilton draws on.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: we shall see examples of such vigorous plants flourishing in the soil of federal as well as of State legislation
matches.shakespearePassage: Cut off the heads of too-fast-growing sprays / That look too lofty in our commonwealth. / All must be even in our government.
matches.explanation: Hamilton's horticultural metaphor resonates with Shakespeare's garden allegory for the state in Richard II 3.4. However, the plant/garden metaphor for politics was classical (Plato, Cicero) long before Shakespeare.

matches.author: Madison
matches.federalistPassage: the most dark and degraded pictures which display the infirmities and depravities of the human character
matches.shakespearePassage: What a piece of work is a man (Hamlet); themes of political assembly failures in Coriolanus and Julius Caesar.
matches.explanation: Madison's meditation on human depravity resonates broadly with Shakespeare's dramatizations. But this is common Enlightenment skepticism drawn more from Calvinist theology and Hume.

matches.author: Madison
matches.federalistPassage: the pestilential influence of party animosities -- the disease most incident to deliberative bodies
matches.shakespearePassage: Domestic fury and fierce civil strife (JC 3.1); A plague on both your houses! (R&J 3.1)
matches.explanation: The characterization of faction as plague and disease parallels Shakespeare's treatment. But the disease metaphor for faction was pervasive in 18th-century political thought (especially Hume).

matches.author: Madison
matches.federalistPassage: the transcendent law of nature and of nature's God
matches.shakespearePassage: Thou, Nature, art my goddess. To thy law / My services are bound.
matches.explanation: A coincidence of vocabulary, not an allusion. Madison invokes natural law in its Lockean sense; Edmund subversively invokes Nature to overturn established custom. The phrase comes from Deist philosophy.

matches.author: Madison
matches.federalistPassage: silently and patiently behold the gathering storm, and continue to supply the materials, until it should be prepared to burst on their own heads
matches.shakespearePassage: Between the acting of a dreadful thing / And the first motion, all the interim is / Like a phantasma
matches.explanation: The "gathering storm" metaphor has Shakespearean resonance, particularly with the storm imagery in Julius Caesar. However, "gathering storm" was common in 18th-century English.

matches.author: Madison
matches.federalistPassage: The legislative department is everywhere extending the sphere of its activity, and drawing all power into its impetuous vortex.
matches.shakespearePassage: Then everything includes itself in power, / Power into will, will into appetite, / And appetite, an universal wolf...Must make perforce an universal prey / And last eat up himself.
matches.explanation: Madison's description of unchecked power consuming everything parallels Ulysses' warning about the collapse of degree. The "impetuous vortex" image parallels Shakespeare's "universal wolf." However, the vortex metaphor was common in 18th-century political rhetoric.

matches.author: Madison
matches.federalistPassage: parchment barriers against the encroaching spirit of power
matches.shakespearePlay: Richard II; Hamlet; Henry VI Part 2
matches.shakespeareLocation: Act 2, Scene 1 (R2); Act 5, Scene 1 (Ham); Act 4, Scene 2 (H6P2)
matches.shakespearePassage: inky blots and rotten parchment bonds (R2); Is not parchment made of sheepskins? (Hamlet)
matches.explanation: Madison's "parchment barriers" resonates with Shakespeare's recurring skepticism about parchment authority. However, skepticism about paper constitutions was common in Montesquieu and others.

matches.author: Disputed (Hamilton or Madison)
matches.federalistPassage: If men were angels, no government would be necessary.
matches.shakespearePassage: man, proud man, / Dressed in a little brief authority, / Most ignorant of what he's most assured, / His glassy essence, like an angry ape / Plays such fantastic tricks before high heaven / As makes the angels weep
matches.explanation: Both passages address the gap between human nature and governance ideals. Isabella contrasts human officials with angels. However, this is a Christian theological commonplace (original sin applied to governance) rather than distinctively Shakespearean.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: There is a contagion in example which few men have sufficient force of mind to resist.
matches.shakespearePlay: Julius Caesar; Henry IV Part 1; Henry V
matches.shakespearePassage: To dare the vile contagion of the night (JC 1.3); the base contagious clouds (1H4 1.2)
matches.explanation: Hamilton's aphoristic use of "contagion" as a political metaphor echoes Shakespeare's frequent disease imagery. But "contagion" as political metaphor was widespread in 18th-century writing.

matches.author: Disputed (Hamilton or Madison)
matches.federalistPassage: The internal effects of a mutable policy are still more calamitous. It poisons the blessing of liberty itself.
matches.shakespearePassage: We have strict statutes and most biting laws...Which for this fourteen years we have let slip...And liberty plucks justice by the nose
matches.explanation: Both passages argue that instability in law corrupts liberty. However, the specific concerns differ (mutability vs. non-enforcement) and the argument was common in Montesquieu.

matches.author: Disputed (Hamilton or Madison)
matches.federalistPassage: Popular liberty might then have escaped the indelible reproach of decreeing to the same citizens the hemlock on one day and statues on the next.
matches.shakespearePassage: In Coriolanus, the Roman populace veers between honoring and banishing the hero. In Julius Caesar, the crowd's allegiance swings between factions within minutes.
matches.explanation: The image of popular fickleness echoes the central dramatic irony of Coriolanus and Julius Caesar. The specific reference is to Athens (Socrates, Phocion), and the author draws on Plutarch rather than Shakespeare.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: He has been shown to us with the diadem sparkling on his brow and the imperial purple flowing in his train.
matches.shakespeareLocation: Henry V 4.1; Titus 1.1; Henry VI plays
matches.shakespearePassage: 'Tis not the balm, the scepter, and the ball (H5); That wore the imperial diadem of Rome (Titus)
matches.explanation: Hamilton's satirical portrait of the president as monarch employs imagery of royal spectacle used extensively in Shakespeare's history plays. However, this imagery was common in 18th-century political satire.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: beset, as they continually are, by the wiles of parasites and sycophants
matches.shakespearePassage: Most smiling, smooth, detested parasites (Timon); Soft as the parasite's silk (Coriolanus)
matches.explanation: The pairing of "parasites and sycophants" echoes Shakespeare's treatment in Timon and Coriolanus. However, this was standard 18th-century political rhetoric deriving from classical vocabulary.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: Would it promote the peace of the community...to have half a dozen men...wandering among the people like discontented ghosts
matches.shakespearePlay: Hamlet; Julius Caesar; Richard III; Macbeth
matches.shakespearePassage: Hamlet's Ghost; Caesar's ghost to Brutus; the parade of ghosts before Richard III; Banquo's ghost.
matches.explanation: Hamilton's image of former presidents as "discontented ghosts" draws on the theatrical image of spirits unable to rest. Shakespeare's ghost scenes are the most famous English literary treatments. However, ghost imagery was commonplace in 18th-century prose.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: the contagion of some common passion or interest
matches.shakespearePlay: Julius Caesar; Henry IV Part 1; Henry V
matches.shakespearePassage: To dare the vile contagion of the night (JC); the base contagious clouds (1H4)
matches.explanation: Hamilton's use of "contagion" as metaphor for factional passion echoes Shakespeare's frequent use of disease imagery in political contexts. However, "contagion" as political metaphor was widespread.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: the pestilential breath of faction may poison the fountains of justice
matches.shakespearePassage: The poisoned fountain clears itself again (Lucrece); a foul and pestilent congregation of vapors (Hamlet)
matches.explanation: Hamilton's vivid image combines two Shakespearean image clusters: pestilence with corruption and poisoned fountains. However, the metaphor of poisoned fountains of justice was common in 18th-century writing, traceable to Cicero and Juvenal.

matches.author: Madison
matches.federalistPassage: a single orator, or an artful statesman, was generally seen to rule with as complete a sway as if a sceptre had been placed in his single hand
matches.shakespearePassage: Antony's manipulation of the Roman crowd (JC 3.2); the tribunes manipulating the plebeians throughout Coriolanus.
matches.explanation: Madison's image of a demagogue wielding a "sceptre" evokes Shakespeare's dramatizations. But Madison's classical references suggest he draws on historical sources (Plutarch, Livy) rather than Shakespeare.

matches.author: Hamilton
matches.federalistPassage: Even the love of fame, the ruling passion of the noblest minds
matches.shakespearePassage: Who can bring noblest minds to basest ends! (Timon)
matches.explanation: Hamilton's phrase has an echo in Timon's "noblest minds," but the concept of a "ruling passion" derives from Alexander Pope's Essay on Man. Over-attribution to Shakespeare would be inappropriate. The immediate source is almost certainly Pope rather than Shakespeare.
